Portugal, officially known as the Portuguese Republic, is a semi-presidential representative democratic republic with Lisbon as its capital and largest city. The country was ruled by António de Oliveira Salazar between 1932 and 1968 under the Estado Novo regime. Since 2016, Marcelo Rebelo de Sousa has served as the President. Portugal is a secular state, with the separation of church and state established during the First Portuguese Republic and reiterated in the 1976 Portuguese Constitution. The oldest Portuguese university was initially founded in Lisbon before relocating to Coimbra, while the University of Lisbon currently holds the title of the largest university in the country.

Portugal, located in the Iberian Peninsula in Southwestern Europe, occupies an area known for having one of the oldest established territorial borders in the continent. Most Portuguese regard the Iberian Peninsula as *the continent*. The country is home to six terrestrial ecoregions and is situated in the Mediterranean Basin, recognized for its diverse flora. Furthermore, Portugal has gained international acclaim, being elected *The Best European Country* by *USA Today* in 2014 and recognized as *Europe's Leading Destination* in 2017 as well as *World's Leading Destination* in both 2018 and 2019. Additionally, Portugal has emerged as a leading golf destination worldwide.

Portugal is a member of several international organizations, including the United Nations, the European Union, the Schengen Area, and the Council of Europe. It was a founding member of NATO, the OECD, and EFTA, before joining the European Economic Community. Additionally, Portugal is part of the eurozone and the Community of Portuguese Language Countries. Furthermore, it is a member of the European Space Agency (ESA) and maintains its membership in NATO and the Community of Portuguese Language Countries.

In Portugal, the legal system is part of the civil law legal tradition, with key laws including the Constitution established in 1976, the Portuguese Civil Code from 1966, and the Penal Code enacted in 1982. The Supreme Court of Justice serves as the highest court in the country, while the Constitutional Court, which oversees the constitutionality of laws, comprises thirteen members and is considered one of the supreme national courts.

The Carnation Revolution in April 1974 marked a significant turning point for Portugal, as it restored democracy and effectively ended the Portuguese Colonial War, leading to the independence of its last African territories. This revolution not only aimed to affirm Portugal's status as a transcontinental nation rather than a colonial empire but also initiated important domestic changes, including land reform and nationalizations.

During the 18th-century gold rush, an estimated 600,000 Portuguese migrants moved to Colonial Brazil, marking a significant migration event. Additionally, following the retreat of overseas colonies, there was a mass exodus of Portuguese citizens from African territories, with over one million Portuguese refugees fleeing the former provinces in Africa. In recent years, Portugal has also experienced an influx of immigrants from various regions, including Ukraine, Brazil, and African countries, contributing to a significant net immigration.

Portugal's armed conflict in Portuguese India against Indian Armed Forces resulted in the defeat of Portuguese forces, leading to the loss of remaining Portuguese territories in the Indian subcontinent. This military setback coincided with a period of heightened independence movements in Portuguese Angola, Portuguese Mozambique, and Portuguese Guinea, which triggered the Portuguese Colonial War from 1961 to 1974. During this tumultuous period, Portugal faced significant dissent and was subjected to arms embargoes.

Portugal joined the European Economic Community in 1986, which significantly boosted its economic progress through European Structural and Investment Funds. This membership granted Portugal easier access to foreign markets and has made the country a net beneficiary of the European Union budget since its accession.

In the eighth century, the Iberian Peninsula, including present-day Portugal, was invaded by the Umayyad Caliphate, which conquered the Visigoths and initiated a rapid expansion starting in 726. Muslims gradually occupied mainland Portugal until they were expelled through the Christian Reconquista, which occurred between 1238 and 1249 AD.

Portugal was initially recognized as a kingdom in 1179, following its emergence as a major maritime power during the Age of Discovery. The foundation of this kingdom was marked by the victory in the Battle of Ourique in 1139, which led to the proclamation of Afonso Henriques as King of Portugal. Afonso Henriques was subsequently recognized as the first king of Portugal in 1143 by King Alfonso VII of León and again in 1179 by Pope Alexander III.

Before the Roman conquest, northern and central Portugal were dominated by a specific culture, while southern Portugal had an older character believed to be non-Indo-European and likely related to the Basque people. Following the Roman era, the Suebi and Visigoths emerged as the dominant Germanic tribes in modern Portugal.
Carvalho e Melo became prime minister in 1755 and was entrusted with more control of the state by the King. Over time, the King's confidence in Carvalho e Melo increased.

After the revolution, Portugal's economic situation prompted the pursuit of International Monetary Fund (IMF)-monitored stabilization programmes. The first of these programmes was implemented from 1977 to 1978, followed by a second programme from 1983 to 1985.

Portugal's climate is diverse, encompassing Mediterranean, temperate maritime, semi-arid, hot desert, and humid subtropical climates, with average temperatures varying significantly across regions. However, climate change is impacting this landscape, leading to rising temperatures and longer-lasting heat waves. Despite these challenges, Portugal also experiences a seasonal upwelling system in the summer, which brings cooler, nutrient-rich water to the sea surface. This process supports the growth of phytoplankton and zooplankton, thus promoting rich marine biodiversity.

Portugal co-founded the Community of Portuguese Language Countries (CPLP), also known as the Lusophone Commonwealth, in 1996. This international organization serves as a political association of nations where Portuguese is recognized as an official language.

Portugal hosted several significant summits in the late 2000s and early 2010s, including the first EU–Brazil summit in July 2007 and the second EU–African Union summit in December 2007. Additionally, Portugal hosted a NATO summit in November 2010.

Portugal is notable for its significant contributions to global agriculture, being the top producer of cork, which accounts for about 50% of world production, and also the leading producer of carob, contributing approximately 30% to the global supply. Additionally, Portugal ranks among the world's top ten largest olive oil producers and stands as the fourth largest exporter of olive oil.

Continental Portugal is served by four international airports located near Lisbon, Porto, Faro, and Beja, with Lisbon particularly acting as a stopover for many foreign airlines. The main airports in the country include those in Lisbon, Porto, Faro, as well as Funchal in Madeira and Ponta Delgada in the Azores, all managed by ANA – Aeroportos de Portugal.

The Lisbon Metro is part of the subway systems serving the Lisbon metropolitan area, while the Metro Sul do Tejo light rail system operates within the same region, providing over 35 km of lines. In addition to these, the Porto Metro light metro system caters to the Porto metropolitan area, also featuring more than 35 km of lines.

In 2023, 12% of Portugal's population, totaling 1,293,463 individuals, consisted of legal resident foreigners. Between 2008 and 2022, 340,000 resident foreigners acquired Portuguese citizenship, with nearly 21,000 foreign residents obtaining citizenship in 2022 alone, comprising 11,170 females and 9,674 males.

The influence of traditional religions on their respective herbal medicine practices is evident in various cultures. In Africa, African Traditional Religion significantly impacts traditional African herbal medicine, shaping the beliefs, practices, and applications of herbal remedies. Similarly, Chinese Traditional Religion plays a crucial role in Traditional Chinese Medicine, where spiritual and cultural elements are intertwined with the use of herbs for healing. This connection between traditional religions and herbal medicine practices highlights how deeply rooted spiritual beliefs can guide medicinal approaches in different cultural contexts.

The health system comprises the National Health Service (SNS), special social health insurance schemes, and voluntary private health insurance. The SNS provides universal coverage, with 55% of the population benefited through health subsystems, 43% through private insurance, and 12% through mutual funds. The Ministry of Health oversees the SNS and develops health policy, while five regional health administrations are tasked with implementing the national policy objectives.

Employer contributions serve as a primary funding source for health subsystems, complemented by employee contributions. Additionally, direct payments made by patients and premiums from voluntary health insurance significantly contribute to the overall funding of these subsystems.

In Portugal, approximately 30,000 deaths per year are attributed to cardiovascular diseases (CVD), which account for one third of all annual deaths. Among these, cerebrovascular disease stands out as the leading cause of death, resulting in over 11,000 fatalities annually, a trend that contrasts with observations in Eur-A countries. Additionally, oncological diseases contribute to 22% of all deaths in Portugal, with a notable prevalence of cervical and prostate cancer, while mortality rates from diabetes have decreased from 2010 to 2021.

Fundação Gulbenkian and Porto National Orchestra are among the most important symphony orchestras in Portugal, while the Teatro Nacional de São Carlos in Lisbon stands out as the country's most representative opera house.

The region came under Roman control in the second century BC, following the Romans' initial invasion of the Iberian Peninsula in 219 BC. During Julius Caesar's rule, the Romans annexed almost the entire peninsula.

The name 'Portugal' originates from the Roman-Celtic place name 'Portus Cale', which is derived from the Latin word 'portus', meaning 'port'. 'Portus Cale' translates to 'Port of Cale' and was incorporated into the province of Gallaecia.
The intermarriage of the Celts with local populations in Portugal resulted in the formation of several different ethnic groups due to Celtic migration.
The Visigoths established a kingdom centered in Toledo, unifying the Iberian Peninsula under their rule.

The Emirate of Córdoba was established by Abd-ar-Rahman I after the western part of the empire gained its independence. It later became the Caliphate of Córdoba in 929 and was ultimately dissolved in 1031 into 23 Taifa kingdoms.
